Compiler, VecTrans successfully vectorizes 23 cases (46%)
and achieves an average speedup of 2.02x, greatly surpassing
state-of-the-art performance.

Vectorization is an critical compiler optimization that trans-
forms scalar operations into vectorized forms to exploit the
parallel processing capabilities of SIMD (Single Instruction,
1

tion Framework for Better
igh-performance CPU
heng  Lu Li

Multiple Data) architectures. Vectorization dramatically im-
proves computational throughput by simultaneously process-
ing multiple data elements as a single vector operation, mak-
ing it indispensable for performance-critical domains such
as scientific computing, machine learning and multimedia
processing. The theoretical foundation of vectorization dates
back to Flynn’s taxonomy of parallel architectures[1], but
its practical implementation has evolved alongside advance-
ments in processor design, particularly with the proliferation
of SIMD instruction sets such as Intel AVX[2], ARM SVE[3],
and RISC-V V Extension[4]. Despite decades of researching
and engineering investments, automatic vectorization still
faces serious challenges, including complex data dependen-
cies and diverse code patterns.
Currently, several strategies are available to address non-
vectorized loops, including manually modifying the source
code, using intrinsic instructions, and applying targeted vec-
torization optimizations. While these methods can be effec-
tive, they each comewith their own set of drawbacks. Manual
source code modifications (e.g., rewriting loops into SIMD-
friendly structures) preserve readability through standard
C/C++ implementations, enabling cross-platform compati-
bility via compiler portability. However, such hand-tuning
demands significant developer effort and risks introducing
subtle errors like incorrect dependency handling. Compiler
intrinsics, e.g., Intel’s _mm512_load_ps, provide precise hard-
ware control, but sacrifice portability by binding code to
specific instruction sets (e.g., AVX-512 intrinsics fail on ARM
platforms) and obfuscating algorithmic intent. Targeted op-
timizations automate some vectorization tasks but struggle
with non-affine loops or dynamic control flow.

2.1  Background
Modern compilers generally employ two primary vectoriza-
tion strategies, namely loop vectorization and Superword
Level Parallelism (SLP) vectorization[10]. Loop vectorization,
the more established approach, transforms entire loop iter-
ations into vector operations by exploiting cross-iteration
parallelism. However, this method requires an extra ver-
ification given that loop-carried dependencies are absent.
In contrast, SLP vectorization operates at the basic block
level, identifying and grouping isomorphic scalar operations
within loop bodies or straight-line code into vector instruc-
tions [11–13]. Both vectorization approaches face significant
limitations when dealing with real-world complex codes.
Loop vectorization inherently fails to handle loop-carried de-
pendencies situations where iteration i+1 depends on results
from iteration i (e.g., recurrence relations like prefix sums).

to provide some accurate feedback information. Besides, they
are more suitable to compile source code precisely on back-
end SIMD instructions than LLM. The further description
about VecTrans are presented in the next section.
3  Design
VecTrans is an iterative refinement LLM optimization frame-
work that uses feedback-refined iteration structures[9]. The

as non-vectorizable, the framework initiates an iterative optimi

self-evaluation feedback from large language models, static anal
The refinement phase then leverages these diagnostic insight

either upon successful vectorization achievement or upon reac
feedback-refined iteration is a cyclical optimization process
comprising two phases: the feedback phase and the refine
phase. During the feedback phase, information relevant to
the optimized function is gathered, referred to as feedback
information. This feedback is utilized to assess the correct-
ness of the optimized function and to identify the potential
for vectorization. In the refine phase, this feedback is applied
to update and enhance the optimized function. The feedback
information is derived from three sources, as illustrated in
Figure 2:
• Source 1: Self-Feedback. The basic feedback infor-
mation comes from the capabilities of the LLM itself.
• Source 2: Compiler Feedback. Feedback informa-
tion from the compiler, including compilation informa-
tion and vectorization information. This information
is used to help the LLM determine whether the cur-
rently generated optimized code can be compiled and
vectorized successfully.
• Source 3: Verification Feedback. The feedback in-
formation is used to check whether the generated
optimized code can pass the unit tests and formal ver-
ification. This helps the large model determine the
semantic correctness of the optimized code.
3.1  Source 1: Self-Feedback
The self-feedback information is derived from the analytical
capabilities of the LLM. Specifically, the model is leveraged
to assess various aspects of the currently optimized code,
including lexical correctness, syntactic accuracy, semantic in-
tegrity, and the effectiveness of vectorization. By conducting

3.2  Source 2: Compiler Feedback
Compiler feedback information consists of three parts as
shown in Figure 3. In the first part, it conducts a compilation-
error check on the optimized code, capitalizing on the error-
related information provided by the compiler. In the second
part, it performs a semantic-equivalence analysis by com-
paring the source code and the optimized code through unit

cache, 128 MB L3 cache). For validation, we employ BiSheng

Table 2 shows the manner in which the LLM transforma-
tion function enables vectorization. Through these transfor-
mations, the compiler can more easily identify whether there
is a dependency between loops, so that the compiler can vec-
torize a function that could not be vectorized originally. One
of the most effective is the loop splitting technology, which
can effectively eliminate many loop dependencies.

In addition to the test cases that can be successfully vec-
torized, there are 27 test cases that cannot be successfully
transformed by the framework. The conversion fails due to
the following reasons:
• The number of iteration rounds is exceeded. The
upper limit of the number of iterations is set to 20.
When the number of iterations exceeds 20, if the LLM
still considers that the function at this time cannot be
automatically vectorized, the iteration is terminated
and the source code is output.
• The LLM considers that the code has been opti-
mized. In some cases, the LLM will ignore the vec-
torization information provided by the compiler. As a
result, the LLM mistakenly considers that the current

function can be automatically vectorized and chooses
to end the iteration and output the source code.
• The LLM considers that there is no optimization
space. The LLM considers that the existing loop de-
pendencymakes the function unable to complete auto-
matic vectorization through the compiler, and chooses
to end the iteration and output the source code.
• Generate wrong code. Among the 51 test functions,
there is only one function s481, and the optimization
function generated by the function s481 can pass the
unit test and formal verification. However, the seman-
tics of the optimization function is different from the
original semantics, mainly because the input coverage
of the unit test is limited. In addition, the formal veri-
fication tool is incomplete. This situation will output
an error code, which is unacceptable, and future work
will attempt to deal with it.
The functions that cannot be successfully converted by
the framework have the following features: two-dimensional
arrays, conditional branches, and function calls. In addition,
if the loop dependency cannot be resolved, the loop cannot
be vectorized successfully. The main factor is the existence
of conditional branches. There are 11 test cases that cannot
be transformed by the framework because of the existence of
conditional branches. Second, there are 8 functions that can-
not be resolved because of loop dependencies.And there are
six functions that cannot be vectorized due to the difficulty
of memory access analysis caused by arrays. The remaining
two test cases are due to function calls, function inlining is
required before the next step is carried out, which is difficult
to implement at the source code level.
4.3  Speedup and Iteration Rounds
Under the Neon instruction set, a total of 24 functions are
successfully vectorized using the BiSheng Compiler[16].

In
comparison to the O3 optimization level, which achieves
an average speedup of 2.02x and a maximum speedup of
5.21x (for function s293), two functions exhibit negative op-
timization outcomes and four functions show minimal or no
performance improvement. This degradation in performance
can be attributed to the additional overhead introduced by
the transformation process, such as the creation of tempo-
rary arrays, cache misses, and other inefficiencies, which
ultimately outweigh the benefits of vectorization.
The optimization framework typically necessitates a mean
of 8.76 iterations per function. Functions exceeding this
threshold undergo optimization cycles up to a maximum
of 20 iterations, with 9 functions reaching this upper bound.
Successful vectorization requires significantly fewer itera-
tions, averaging 4.96. Each iteration round comprises two dis-
tinct Large Language Model (LLM) interactions: the feedback
phase, where performance data is collected and analyzed,
8

and the refinement phase, where the optimization function
is updated based on the feedback.

4.4  Breakdown Analysis
To understand the impact of the different components of the
framework on VecTrans, we evaluated the impact of formal
verification, compiler feedback, and unit test on the overall
framework.
Table 3 shows the coverage and number of iteration rounds
under different configurations. It can be seen that when a
component is removed, the coverage decreases, and when all
three components are removed (Base Model), the coverage
reaches the lowest value. This reflects the positive effect of
all three components in helping the framework complete
code conversion. Compiler feedback has the greatest impact,
and formal validation has the least impact.
Compiler feedback helps the entire framework in three
ways as follows.
• Helps LLM determine whether a compilation er-
ror occurs. If a compilation error occurs, the opti-
mized function needs to be regenerated.
• Helps LLM determine whether vectorization is
successful. If vectorization has not been performed,
LLM needs analyze whether vectorization opportuni-
ties exist and provide conversion suggestions.
• Helps with LLM analysis which transformation
can facilitate automatic vectorization by the com-
piler. The compiler gives the loop position that can-
not be vectorized and the reason why it cannot be
vectorized.

Therefore, when the compiler feedback is removed, the
LLM needs to rely on its analysis capability to determine
whether the current optimized function can be automatically
vectorized. When the LLM mistakenly considers that the
current function can be automatically vectorized, the LLM
chooses to end the iteration and output code. In fact, the cur-
rent function is not successfully vectorized. In experiments,
this is common, so when the compiler feedback is removed,
the coverage decreases, and the number of iteration rounds
decreases, because the LLM considers that the current op-
timized function has been fully optimized in the first few
iteration rounds and selects to output the code.
Unit test and formal validation are similar in that they are
used to detect the semantic correctness of the generated code,
where unit test is more important than formal verification
in that it can intercept more incorrect code because formal
verification will fail in some complex situations. The unit test
is more applicable, but it cannot cover all cases due to the
limited input range. Therefore, formal verification is needed
as a supplement.
The removal of different components has an impact on the
coverage rate and iteration rounds, but has little impact on
the acceleration ratio because the code optimization meth-
ods used by the same LLM are similar and the generated
code is basically the same. Except that a few functions may
reduce the acceleration ratio due to redundant operations,
the acceleration ratio remains the same in other cases.
4.5  Sensitivity Studies
We verify the robustness of the framework from three di-
mensions: model, compiler, and architecture.

Only one pa-
rameter is changed at a time, and the rest remain unchanged.
We have verified that the framework works across different
LLMs, compilers, and architectures.

Sensitivity of different models. We evaluate the us-
ability of the framework on three LLMs, Qwen2.5-Coder-
32B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-72B-Instruct, and Deepseek-V3. In
addition, small-scale tests are performed on smaller mod-
els. It is found that the instruction compliance capability
of smaller models is poor and the normal iteration process
cannot be completed. The Deepseek-R1 model is also tested
on a small scale, which can produce correct results in fewer
iterations, but takes too long.
The coverage rate and iteration rounds of the three dif-
ferent base models are shown in Table 3. It can be seen that
with the decrease of model parameters, the coverage rate
decreases and the number of iteration rounds increases. This
is because the decrease of model parameters leads to the
weakening of model capabilities, so some test functions are
difficult to successfully transform. At the same time, more
iteration rounds are required, and some test functions cannot
output correct results within 20 iteration rounds..

This section provides an overview of related work on LLM
for compiler.
Automatic vectorization. The research on automatic
vectorization has been a hot topic. Franchetti and Püschel[22]
use the term rewriting technique to vectorize small matrix
kernels. Thomas et al.[23] use equality saturation to automat-
ically generate high-quality vectorizing compiler for digital
signal processors, creating a new idea for automatic vector-
ization.
LLM for compiler. In recent years, there has been a
lot of work using LLMs to optimize compilers, focusing on
different aspects of code work. Armengol-Estapé et al.[24]
demonstrate an innovative decompilation using a small lan-
guage model to ensure correctness while improving code
readability. Program repair is another study that has been
highlighted. Bouzenia et al.[25] present a method of using
LLMs to automatically repair programs, using iterative in-
teraction, and shows how large models interact with tools
.Iterative interaction framework[26][9] is widely used in
LLM applications to improve the ability of generation. In ad-
dition to optimizing code directly with LLM, another idea is
to generate transform pass that can optimize code. Cummins
et al.[27] use the way of chain of thought to generate trans-
form pass, thus improving the accuracy of LLM optimization.
In addition, the LLM is used to predict the pass list, which is
another way to avoid code correctness verification[28]. By
adjusting the pass list, the performance of the program can
be greatly changed.
ML for vectorization. Machine learning techniques are
now being used to improve automatic vectorization. Kevin
et al.[29] replace the cost model of automatic vectorization
with a machine learning model to help the compiler make
vectorization decisions. Ameer et al.[30] deploy a reinforce-
ment learning method in the compiler to predict the optimal
vectorization factor, which improves the performance of

We implement a code transformation (source to souce) frame-
work based on LLMs (large language models), which can
effectively transform source code into a form that is more
friendly for automatic vectorization. This framework gives
full play to the ability of automatic vectorization of compiler.
Furthermore, it uses the unit test and formal verification
tools to ensure the correctness of generated code. This com-
bination of the LLM and compiler reduces the responsibility
of LLM, thus decreasing the probability of error. The pro-
posed framework makes full use of the inference ability of
LLMs, while preserving the accuracy of the traditional com-
pilation vectorization. This combination will provide hints
for future research. In future work, we will further ensure
that the generated code is correct and extend the LLM to
more compilation optimizations.
